target.name: (+)-Randainin D
target.motivation: Randainin D (3) is a structurally unique diterpenoid with a trans-hydroazulenone core and a C9-butenolide moiety. It inhibits elastase release and superoxide-anion generation, suggesting therapeutic potential for conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, cystic fibrosis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and COVID-19. The synthesis aims to achieve its first total synthesis and establish a general strategy for trans-cycloheptane-containing polycyclic terpenes.

success_criteria.free_text: First total synthesis, enantioselective, confirmation of absolute configuration.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the butenolide moiety
key_disconnections.reasoning: To install the C9-butenolide moiety, a novel deoxygenative allylation under Ir-photoredox catalysis was envisioned, employing methyl oxalate as a red/ox tag. This strategy forms a key Cq-C(sp3) bond.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the cycloheptenone ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: To form the trans-5/7 bicyclic skeleton, RCM was planned, inspired by its successful application in other syntheses, despite the challenge of forming a tetrasubstituted cycloheptenone.
To introduce the hydroxyl group at C9, a regioselective Mukaiyama hydration of a tetrasubstituted enone was planned.
To install the C5-isopentenyl moiety, a 1,2-addition to a ketone was planned.
To introduce the C1-side chain, a diastereoselective alpha-hydroxymethylation of a cyclopentanone was planned.
To achieve enantioselectivity and desymmetrize the commercially available 2,2-dimethylcyclopentane-1,3-dione, an asymmetric transfer hydrogenation was planned.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The retrosynthetic analysis aimed for an enantioselective total synthesis of randainin D (3) by strategically employing a novel photoredox-catalyzed deoxygenative allylation for the butenolide moiety and a challenging ring-closing metathesis for the hydroazulenone core. The synthesis starts from a commercially available dione, with early introduction of enantioselectivity and careful control of diastereoselectivity throughout the build-up of the complex polycyclic system.

thought.background: Commercially available 2,2-dimethylcyclopentane-1,3-dione (10) is a suitable starting material for the cyclopentanone core. Desymmetrization is required to achieve enantioselectivity.
thought.gap: Achieving high enantioselectivity in the desymmetrization of 10.
thought.decision: To desymmetrize 10 with high enantioselectivity, chose asymmetric transfer hydrogenation using (R,R)-Ts-DENEB and formic acid as the hydrogen donor, expecting excellent enantioselectivity and good yield. The resulting alcohol is then protected as a TBDPS ether.

outcome.observation: Desired TBDPS-protected ketone 9 was synthesized on a multigram scale in 79% yield over 2 steps with excellent enantioselectivity (e.r. 99.4:0.6).

evidence.text_span: Indeed, using commercially available (R,R)-Ts-DENEB and formic acid as the hydrogen donor, we synthesized the desired TBDPS-protected ketone 9 on a multigram scale in 79% yield over 2 steps with excellent enantioselectivity (e. r. 99.4:0.6).

thought.background: Ketone 9 is available. The next step is to install the C1-side chain.
thought.gap: Direct alkylation/isomerization under kinetic control to install the C1-side chain without unnecessary red/ox manipulations.
thought.decision: Attempted direct alkylation/isomerization under kinetic control.

outcome.observation: Initial efforts to install the C1-side chain via alkylation/isomerization under kinetic control to minimize unnecessary red/ox manipulations were unsuccessful.

evidence.text_span: Our initial efforts to install the C1-side chain via alkylation/isomerization under kinetic control to minimize unnecessary red/ox manipulations were unsuccessful.

thought.background: Direct alkylation/isomerization failed. An alternative strategy is needed for the C1-side chain.
thought.gap: Finding a reliable method for diastereoselective alpha-hydroxymethylation.
thought.decision: To install the C1-side chain, chose a 4-step sequence starting with SET-based dehydrogenation to form an enone, followed by Morita-Baylis-Hillman reaction, TES protection, and hydrogenation.

evidence.text_span: Instead, the diastereoselective α-hydroxymethylation of ketone 9 to furnish 8 was accomplished through a 4-step sequence.

thought.background: Ketone 9 needs to be converted to an enone for the subsequent Morita-Baylis-Hillman reaction.
thought.gap: Efficiently forming the enone from 9.
thought.decision: To form the enone, chose SET-based dehydrogenation mediated by the IBX/NMO complex, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Enone 11 was delivered in 79% yield on a 14 g scale.

evidence.text_span: First, 9 was subjected to single-electron transfer (SET)-based dehydrogenation mediated by the IBX/NMO complex,15 delivering the enone 11 in 79% yield on a 14 g scale.

thought.background: Enone 11 is available. The next step is to introduce the hydroxymethyl group.
thought.gap: Performing a Morita-Baylis-Hillman reaction with aqueous formaldehyde.
thought.decision: To introduce the hydroxymethyl group, chose Morita-Baylis-Hillman reaction of 11 with aqueous formaldehyde, catalyzed by tributyl phosphine, expecting high yield.

outcome.observation: The desired beta-hydroxy ketone 12 was afforded in 95% yield on a 10 g scale.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent Morita−Baylis−Hillman reaction of 11 with aqueous formaldehyde, catalyzed by tributyl phosphine,16 afforded the desired β-hydroxy ketone 12 in 95% yield on a 10 g scale.

thought.background: Beta-hydroxy ketone 12 is available. The next steps are protection and hydrogenation to form the cyclopentanone 8.
thought.gap: Ensuring diastereoselectivity and preventing OTES cleavage during hydrogenation.
thought.decision: To form 8, chose introduction of the TES-protecting group, followed by hydrogenation. Sodium acetate was used as a buffer base to prevent in situ OTES cleavage. The resulting diastereomeric mixture was separated by column chromatography.

outcome.observation: Ketone 8 was obtained as a mixture of two C1-diastereomers (d.r. 9.7:1). The mixture was separated via column chromatography, delivering pure (R,R)-disubstituted ketone 8 in 77% yield from alcohol 12. Sodium acetate was crucial to prevent in situ OTES cleavage.

evidence.text_span: Introduction of the TES-protecting group, followed by hydrogenation, afforded 8 as a mixture of two C1-diastereomers (d. r. 9.7:1). Sodium acetate, used as a buffer base,17 was crucial to prevent the in situ OTES cleavage during the hydrogenation. The obtained diastereomeric mixture was separated via column chromatography, delivering pure (R,R)-disubstituted ketone 8 in 77% yield from alcohol 12.

thought.background: Ketone 8 is available. The C5-isopentenyl moiety needs to be installed.
thought.gap: Efficiently adding the sterically hindered (3-methylbut-3-en-1-yl)magnesium bromide to ketone 8.
thought.decision: To install the C5-isopentenyl moiety, chose treatment of ketone 8 with freshly prepared (3-methylbut-3-en-1-yl)magnesium bromide in the presence of the LaCl3·2LiCl complex, expecting nearly quantitative yield.

outcome.observation: The C5-isopentenyl moiety was successfully installed in nearly quantitative yield.

evidence.text_span: Then, the C5-isopentenyl moiety was successfully installed in nearly quantitative yield upon treatment of the sterically hindered ketone 8 with freshly prepared (3-methylbut-3-en-1-yl)magnesium bromide in the presence of the LaCl3·2LiCl complex.

thought.background: Compound 13 is available. The primary TES group needs deprotection and oxidation to an aldehyde.
thought.gap: Performing deprotection and oxidation in one pot without epimerization or low yields.
thought.decision: Attempted one-pot deprotection of the primary TES group and oxidation using intrinsically acidic oxidants.

outcome.observation: Attempts to perform this reaction using intrinsically acidic oxidants resulted in lower yields and caused epimerization of the stereocenter adjacent to the carbonyl group.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, attempts to perform this reaction using intrinsically acidic oxidants resulted in lower yields and caused epimerization of the stereocenter adjacent to the carbonyl group (see Supporting Information).

thought.background: Intrinsically acidic oxidants caused epimerization during deprotection/oxidation. A milder approach is needed.
thought.gap: Finding a suitable oxidant for one-pot deprotection and oxidation to aldehyde 14 without epimerization.
thought.decision: To obtain aldehyde 14, chose one-pot deprotection of the primary TES group and oxidation in the presence of an excess of IBX in wet acetone.

evidence.text_span: Next, the one-pot deprotection of the primary TES group and oxidation in the presence of an excess of IBX in wet acetone delivered aldehyde 14 in 83% yield.

thought.background: Compound 13 is available. The primary TES group needs deprotection and oxidation to an aldehyde.
thought.gap: Efficiently forming aldehyde 14 without epimerization.
thought.decision: To obtain aldehyde 14, chose one-pot deprotection of the primary TES group and oxidation in the presence of an excess of IBX in wet acetone, expecting good yield and no epimerization.

outcome.observation: Aldehyde 14 was delivered in 83% yield.

evidence.text_span: Next, the one-pot deprotection of the primary TES group and oxidation in the presence of an excess of IBX in wet acetone delivered aldehyde 14 in 83% yield.

thought.background: Aldehyde 14 is available. The next step is to add isopropenyllithium and then oxidize to form diene 7.
thought.gap: Generating isopropenyllithium and performing the 1,2-addition efficiently.
thought.decision: Attempted to generate isopropenyllithium through t-BuLi-mediated lithiation of 2-bromo- or 2-iodoprop-1-ene.

outcome.observation: Attempts to generate the corresponding organolithium species through the t-BuLi-mediated lithiation of 2-bromo- or 2-iodoprop-1-ene resulted in poor reproducibility.

evidence.text_span: Attempts to generate the corresponding organolithium species through the t-BuLi-mediated lithiation of 2-bromo- or 2-iodoprop-1-ene resulted in poor reproducibility.

thought.background: Aldehyde 14 is available. The next step is to add isopropenyllithium and then oxidize to form diene 7. Previous attempts to generate isopropenyllithium were unsuccessful.
thought.gap: Finding a reliable method for 1,2-addition of an isopropenyl group.
thought.decision: Attempted to use the corresponding Grignard reagent instead of isopropenyllithium for the 1,2-addition.

outcome.observation: When the corresponding Grignard reagent was used instead of isopropenyllithium, 1,2-addition proceeded with low yields (~50%) and led to the formation of side products, presumably due to beta-hydride transfer, despite the presence of lanthanide salts.

evidence.text_span: When the corresponding Grignard reagent was used instead of isopropenyllithium, 1,2-addition proceeded with low yields (~50%) and led to the formation of side products, presumably due to β-hydride transfer, despite the presence of lanthanide salts.

thought.background: Previous attempts to generate isopropenyllithium and use Grignard reagents for 1,2-addition were problematic. A more reliable method is needed.
thought.gap: Efficiently generating isopropenyllithium and performing the 1,2-addition to aldehyde 14, followed by oxidation to diene 7.
thought.decision: To obtain diene 7, chose to generate isopropenyllithium via transmetalation from tetra(isopropenyl)stannane upon treatment with n-BuLi, followed by LaCl3·2LiCl-assisted 1,2-addition to aldehyde 14, and then DMP oxidation.

evidence.text_span: Isopropenyllithium was generated via transmetalation from tetra(isopropenyl)stannane upon treatment of the latter with n-BuLi in THF at −78 °C.19

thought.background: Aldehyde 14 is available. A reliable method for 1,2-addition and oxidation has been identified.
thought.gap: Synthesizing diene 7 efficiently.
thought.decision: To synthesize diene 7, chose LaCl3·2LiCl-assisted 1,2-addition of in situ-generated isopropenyllithium to aldehyde 14, followed by DMP oxidation, expecting good yield over two steps.

outcome.observation: Diene 7 was obtained in 87% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Then, LaCl3·2LiCl-assisted 1,2-addition of i-C3H5Li to aldehyde 14, followed by DMP oxidation, led to the desired diene 7 in 87% yield over 2 steps.

thought.background: Diene 7 is available. The next key step is Ring-Closing Metathesis (RCM) to form the trans-hydroazulenone core.
thought.gap: Performing RCM to generate a tetrasubstituted cycloheptenone, which is known to be challenging.
thought.decision: To form the trans-hydroazulenone 6, chose RCM of 7 with Hoveyda-Grubbs second-generation catalyst (HG-II) in toluene at 110 °C.

outcome.observation: The desired trans-hydroazulenone 6 was obtained in 36% yield, along with 48% of the recovered starting material. HG-II was found to be the most efficient among tested catalysts.

evidence.text_span: To our delight, when 7 was treated with 10 mol % of Hoveyda−Grubbs second-generation catalyst (HG-II) in toluene at 110 °C, the desired trans-hydroazulenone 6 was obtained in 36% yield, along with 48% of the recovered starting material. Several commercially available RCM catalysts, including the molybdenum-based Shrock catalyst, were evaluated, showing that the HG-II was the most efficient (see Supporting Information).

thought.background: RCM of 7 to 6 yielded 36% in toluene, with significant recovered starting material. Optimization is needed.
thought.gap: Improving the yield of the RCM reaction.
thought.decision: To improve RCM yield, chose to optimize the solvent. Cyclopentyl methyl ether (CPME) was selected.

evidence.text_span: Further optimization revealed a significant solvent influence on the reaction outcome. Indeed, when cyclopentyl methyl ether (CPME) was used with 10 mol % of HG-II at 110 °C, product 6 was obtained in 63% yield, together with 27% of the recovered starting material 7. After a single recycling, the combined yield of 6 reached 79%.

thought.background: Diene 7 is available. The RCM conditions have been optimized.
thought.gap: Efficiently forming the trans-hydroazulenone core.
thought.decision: To form the trans-hydroazulenone 6, chose RCM of 7 with Hoveyda-Grubbs second-generation catalyst (HG-II) in CPME at 110 °C, expecting improved yield.

outcome.observation: Product 6 was obtained in 63% yield, together with 27% of the recovered starting material 7. After a single recycling, the combined yield of 6 reached 79%.

evidence.text_span: Indeed, when cyclopentyl methyl ether (CPME) was used with 10 mol % of HG-II at 110 °C, product 6 was obtained in 63% yield, together with 27% of the recovered starting material 7. After a single recycling, the combined yield of 6 reached 79%.

thought.background: Trans-hydroazulenone 6 is available. The next step is C9-regioselective Mukaiyama hydration.
thought.gap: Achieving regioselective hydration at C9.
thought.decision: To perform C9-regioselective Mukaiyama hydration, chose Mn(dpm)3 and an excess of phenylsilane under an oxygen atmosphere, expecting high yield.

outcome.observation: C9-regioselective Mukaiyama hydration was performed in 90% yield.

evidence.text_span: The remaining transformation en route to oxalate 5 was the C9-regioselective Mukaiyama hydration, which was performed in 90% yield by using Mn(dpm)3 and an excess of phenylsilane under an oxygen atmosphere.

thought.background: Alpha-hydroxyketone 15 is available. The cesium semioxalate moiety needs to be introduced to form oxalate 5.
thought.gap: Efficiently converting 15 to oxalate 5.
thought.decision: To introduce the cesium semioxalate moiety, chose the 2-step sequence reported by Overman.

outcome.observation: The cesium semioxalate moiety was introduced via the 2-step sequence reported by Overman.

evidence.text_span: Finally, the cesium semioxalate moiety was introduced via the 2-step sequence reported by Overman (see the Supporting Information).

thought.background: Oxalate 5 is available. The butenolide moiety needs to be installed via Giese coupling.
thought.gap: Successfully performing the Giese coupling between 5 and 4.
thought.decision: Attempted Giese coupling between 5 and 4 under the conditions developed by Overman and MacMillan.

outcome.observation: The Giese coupling delivered the protodeoxygenated ketone 17 as the main product in 54% yield. Only a trace amount of the desired butenolide 16 was observed. Attempts to conduct this transformation under water-free conditions or in the presence of other heteroleptic Ir(III) catalysts were not fruitful.

evidence.text_span: Unfortunately, the Giese coupling between 5 and 4 under the conditions developed by Overman and MacMillan delivered the protodeoxygenated ketone 17 as the main product in 54% yield (Scheme 2a, left). Only a trace amount of the desired butenolide 16 was observed. Attempts to conduct this transformation under water-free conditions or in the presence of other heteroleptic IrIII catalysts were not fruitful.

thought.background: The Giese coupling failed, yielding protodeoxygenated ketone 17. The Ir(III)-reductive cycle seems incompatible with alpha-keto radicals. A different approach for Cq-C(sp3) bond formation is needed.
thought.gap: Developing a photoredox-catalyzed allylation that can trap the alpha-keto radical with an allylstannane.
thought.decision: To form the desired alpha-quaternary ketone, envisioned that the alpha-keto radical generated under blue-light-promoted Ir(III)/Ir(IV) catalysis could be trapped with an allylstannane, by analogy with Pereyre-Keck allylation. Focused on using alkyl oxalates to generate tertiary carbon-centered radicals via an Ir(III)-oxidative catalytic cycle.

evidence.text_span: Nevertheless, we envisioned that, by analogy with the Pereyre−Keck allylation,24 the α-keto radical generated under blue-light-promoted IrIII/IrIV catalysis could be trapped with an allylstannane, leading to the desired α-quaternary ketone. ... Inspired by these precedents, we focused on the use of alkyl oxalates to generate tertiary carbon-centered radicals via an IrIII-oxidative catalytic cycle.29,30

thought.background: Alpha-hydroxyketone 15 is available. A new strategy for Cq-C(sp3) bond formation involves converting 15 to methyl oxalate 18.
thought.gap: Efficiently preparing methyl oxalate 18 from 15.
thought.decision: To prepare methyl oxalate 18, chose treatment of alpha-hydroxyketone 15 with methyl chlorooxoacetate and triethylamine in dichloromethane, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Compound 18 was prepared in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: Compound 18 was prepared in 91% yield by treatment of α-hydroxyketone 15, methyl chlorooxoacetate, and triethylamine in dichloromethane (see the Supporting Information).

thought.background: Methyl oxalate 18 is available. The new photoredox-catalyzed allylation strategy needs to be tested.
thought.gap: Performing the allylation with allyltributylstannane.
thought.decision: To test the allylation, chose irradiation of a mixture containing oxalate 18, allyltributylstannane, and fac-Ir(ppy)3 at 456 nm in DMA, expecting the corresponding allylated product.

outcome.observation: The corresponding allylated product 19 was furnished in 47% yield. The steric hindrance imposed by the two axial silyl groups efficiently shields the bottom face of radical Y•, which exclusively furnishes the C9-diastereomer 19 with the allyl group in the equatorial position.

evidence.text_span: Irradiation of the reaction mixture containing oxalate 18, allyltributylstannane, and fac-Ir(ppy)3 at 456 nm in DMA furnished the corresponding allylated product 19 in 47% yield (Scheme 2a, right). The steric hindrance imposed by the two axial silyl groups efficiently shields the bottom face of radical Y•, which exclusively furnishes the C9-diastereomer 19 with the allyl group in the equatorial position.

thought.background: Allylation with allyltributylstannane yielded 47%. Improvement is desired.
thought.gap: Increasing the yield of the allylation reaction.
thought.decision: To increase the yield, chose to use allyltriphenylstannane instead of allyltributylstannane.

evidence.text_span: The yield was further increased to 51% when allyltriphenylstannane (5 equiv) was employed.

thought.background: Methyl oxalate 18 is available. The optimized allylation conditions need to be applied for the synthesis of randainin D using dihydrofuranone-based allyltin reagent 20.
thought.gap: Successfully performing the allylation and subsequent double-bond conjugation to form C9-butenolide-bearing ketone 16.
thought.decision: To synthesize 16, chose to irradiate methyl oxalate 18 and an excess of 20 in the presence of fac-Ir(ppy)3, followed by treatment of the crude mixture with DBU to promote double-bond conjugation.

outcome.observation: The desired C9-butenolide-bearing ketone 16 was provided in 26% yield over two steps. The initial allylation led to a mixture of isomeric olefins (E/Z) and constitutional isomers, which were then conjugated by DBU.

evidence.text_span: Methyl oxalate 18 and an excess of 20 were irradiated (456 nm,) in the presence of fac-Ir(ppy)3 (Scheme 2b), which led to a mixture of isomeric olefins (E/Z) and constitutional isomers). The crude mixture was treated with DBU in dichloromethane to promote double-bond conjugation,31 hence providing the desired C9-butenolide-bearing ketone 16 in 26% yield over two steps.

thought.background: C9-butenolide-bearing ketone 16 is available. The final step is global deprotection to yield (+)-randainin D (3).
thought.gap: Efficiently deprotecting all silyl groups.
thought.decision: To obtain (+)-randainin D (3), chose global deprotection of the silyl groups with aqueous HF in CH3CN.

outcome.observation: Global deprotection afforded (+)-randainin D (3), the physicochemical properties of which fully matched those reported. This work allows confirmation of the proposed absolute configuration for randainin D.

evidence.text_span: Global deprotection of the silyl groups with aqueous HF in CH3CN afforded (+)-randainin D (3), the physicochemical properties of which fully matched those reported. Overall, randainin D was obtained in 17 steps in 4.4% yield from cyclopentanedione 10. Of note, the current work allows confirmation of the proposed absolute configuration for randainin D, which was not determined in the isolation paper.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for (+)-randainin D from cyclopentanedione 10.

validation.significance: This is the first enantioselective total synthesis of (+)-randainin D, confirming its absolute configuration. The synthesis features a novel Ir(III)-photocatalyzed deoxygenative allylation for Cq-C(sp3) bond formation and a challenging RCM for the trans-hydroazulenone core, expanding synthetic strategies for complex polycyclic systems.

source.title: Total Synthesis of the Diterpenes (+)-Randainin D and (+)-Barekoxide via Photoredox-Catalyzed Deoxygenative Allylation.
